slides.title: Slide 1
slides.speakerNotes:
Good morning everyone.
Welcome to Rookie Academy.

slides.title: Slide 2
Slide 3
slides.speakerNotes:
Main Point
"Businesses don't exist because of products."
"Businesses exist because of problems."
Pause.
"Every successful company in the world began by solving a problem."
Examples
Apple
Didn't build an iPhone.
They solved
'Technology is too complicated.'
Grab
Didn't build an app.
They solved
'Finding safe transportation is difficult.'
Airbnb
Didn't build a website.
They solved
'Hotels are expensive and unavailable.'
AIA
Doesn't sell insurance.
We solve
families losing income
retirement insecurity
education funding
unexpected illness
wealth preservation
Connect to their assignment
"Last week, I didn't ask you to sell."
"I asked you to listen."
Pause.
"You spoke with five people."
"Today, we're going to discover what they were really telling you."
Core Principle
(point to the title)
Problems Before Products.
Never start with
"What can I sell?"
Always begin with
"What problem is this person trying to solve?"
Set Up the Next Slide
"Let's see what you actually heard."
"What worries kept coming up in your conversations?"
That naturally leads into the debrief slide where participants share insights from their five interviews.

slides.title: Vitality Dance
slides.speakerNotes:
Patterns reveal opportunities.
The more often you hear the same concern...
...the more likely it is that this is a real need in the market.
And where there is a real need...
There is an opportunity to create value.

slides.title: Slide 5
slides.speakerNotes:
CHOOSE
ONE PROBLEM.
Subtitle
You can't solve everything.
Start with one important problem for one specific group of people.
(Simple target icon or spotlight icon. Nothing else.)
Narrative
This is probably the most important lesson of Week 2.
Opening
"You probably heard many different concerns."
Education.
Retirement.
Hospital bills.
Savings.
Debt.
Income protection.
Investments.
Business continuity.
Pause.
The Mistake
"Many new advisors think they have to solve everything."
They become...
Financial planner.
Investment advisor.
Insurance expert.
Retirement specialist.
Estate planner.
Business consultant.
All at once.
Pause.
The Reality
(point to the title)
Choose ONE problem.
Because people remember specialists.
Not generalists.
Example
Imagine two introductions.
Advisor A says:
"I help with insurance, investments, retirement, education, savings, health, business, estate planning..."
Pause.
Advisor B says:
"I help young parents protect their family's income."
Which one is easier to remember?
Which one sounds more focused?
Entrepreneur Connection
"Every successful business begins by solving one problem exceptionally well."
Amazon
Books.
Google
Search.
Facebook
College networking.
Grab
Transportation.
They expanded later.
They didn't start by solving everything.
Transition
"Now I'd like each of you to choose the ONE problem that excites you the most."
That leads naturally into the Customer Discovery Canvas.

slides.title: Slide 6
slides.speakerNotes:
Workshop Narrative
"Choose Your One Problem"
Estimated Time: 30–40 minutes
Introduction
"Every squad already has something very valuable."
"You already know who you want to serve."
Pause.
"Today, we're deciding what problem you want to become known for solving."
Set the Context
"Remember..."
"You are NOT choosing a product."
You're choosing a problem.
Products will change.
Solutions will evolve.
Technology will evolve.
But important human problems remain.
Step 1 – Problem Wall (10 minutes)
(Point to the left side.)
"Take one sticky note."
"Write one problem you repeatedly heard during your interviews."
One idea.
One sticky note.
Keep writing until you've listed every recurring problem.
Examples:
Hospital bills
Children's education
Retirement
Loss of income
Emergency savings
Debt
Business continuity
No discussion yet.
Just capture everything.
Step 2 – Find the Pattern (5 minutes)
"Now step back."
"Which problems appear more than once?"
Cluster similar sticky notes together.
Education.
Income protection.
Retirement.
Savings.
Medical costs.
Ask:
"What keeps showing up?"
Step 3 – The Elimination (5 minutes)
"Now comes the difficult part."
Remove the weaker ideas.
Leave only three.
Then two.
Then one.
Pause.
"If your business could only solve ONE problem..."
"Which one would you choose?"
Step 4 – Why Does It Matter? (10 minutes)
Move to the right panel.
Ask:
"If this problem is never solved..."
What happens?
Write one consequence per sticky note.
Not features.
Not products.
Real human consequences.
Examples:
Family loses income.
Children stop schooling.
Dreams are delayed.
Retirement becomes impossible.
Debt increases.
Stress affects relationships.
Business closes.
Ask them to keep asking:
"Then what happens?"
This uncovers the deeper emotional impact.
The Coaching Moment
Walk around the room and challenge each squad.
Don't ask:
"What product would you recommend?"
Instead ask:
"Why is this problem worth dedicating your career to solving?"
That question changes the conversation from selling to purpose.
Final Decision
Each squad writes one sentence.
We help __________________________ overcome __________________________ because __________________________.
Example:
We help young families protect their income because one unexpected illness or death should never destroy their children's future.
Don't worry about making it perfect.
It will evolve in Week 3.
Debrief
Invite each squad to stand.
One minute each.
Complete only this sentence:
"The one problem we chose is..."
Then...
"It matters because..."
No explanations beyond that.
No product discussion.
No feedback yet.
Just listen.
Closing
End with this message:
"Today, you made the first strategic decision of your business."
Pause.
"Not what you'll sell..."
"But what you'll become known for solving."
Bridge to Week 3
Finish with:
"Next week, we'll answer the next question."
"If this is the problem we're solving... how do we build a business around it?"

slides.title: Slide 7
